ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coconut milk food product class; no specific flavoured or reduced-sugar variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW represents coconut milk as a beverage category; flavoured reduced-sugar variant mapped as closest defensible code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured reduced-sugar coconut milk beverage; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve carton of flavoured coconut milk (less sugar) is approximately 200 ml, equivalent to 200 g, commonly consumed as a ready-to-drink beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Coconut Milk (Less Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Flavoured Coconut Milk (Less Sugar), CR ID F01020004. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk composition profile (approximately 86% of total fat is saturated in coconut milk) using USDA FoodData Central coconut milk reference (FDC ID 170172) as a secondary cross-reference for fatty acid breakdown, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: Coconut milk beverage, flavoured — analogous GI estimate based on sweetened plant-based milk beverages; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for flavoured coconut milk (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 (low category) based on analogous sweetened plant-based milk beverages with similar sugar content (~5–6 g/100 g) and fat content (~4–5 g/100 g). The fat content of coconut milk slows gastric emptying, moderating the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (40 × 11.84) / 100 = 4.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar (5.64 g/100 g) and saturated fat (estimated 3.97 g/100 g); sodium is low at 27 mg/100 g but sugar-sweetened beverages independently elevate blood pressure risk (AHA 2018).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this product due to its added sugar content, which independently raises blood pressure risk; choose unsweetened coconut milk (0 g added sugar) as a lower-risk alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 5.64 g/100 g in a liquid matrix with only 0.23 g/100 g dietary fibre; liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes, and the saturated fat from coconut milk may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this sugar-sweetened coconut milk beverage; if coconut milk is desired, select unsweetened varieties with 0 g added sugar and consume in moderation given the high saturated fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of 3.97 g/100 g (predominantly lauric acid from coconut milk) combined with 5.64 g/100 g added sugar; regular consumption of both saturated fat and added sugar is associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this product due to its high saturated fat and added sugar content; unsweetened, lower-fat plant-based milk alternatives such as oat milk or soy milk are preferable for cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated 3.97 g/100 g saturated fat and 5.64 g/100 g added sugar with negligible anti-inflammatory nutrients; saturated fat and added sugar are pro-inflammatory dietary components.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should choose unsweetened coconut milk in moderation and consider anti-inflammatory additions such as turmeric or ginger; this flavoured, sweetened product provides no anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Coconut Milk (Less Sugar) is a commercially processed, plant-based dairy alternative beverage made from coconut milk with added flavouring agents and a reduced sugar formulation. It is dairy-free, vegan, and gluten-free, making it suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals and those following plant-based diets. The product has a creamy, mildly sweet coconut flavour with a smooth, liquid texture typical of ready-to-drink coconut milk beverages. Per 100 g, it provides 69 kcal, 0.94 g protein, 4.62 g fat (predominantly saturated fat estimated at ~3.97 g from coconut milk's lauric acid profile), 5.92 g carbohydrate, and 5.64 g sugar, with a low sodium content of 27 mg. Dietary fibre is minimal at 0.23 g/100 g. Despite the 'less sugar' label, the product still contains 5.64 g sugar per 100 g, which classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA and AHA clinical guidelines. The high saturated fat content from coconut milk raises concerns for cardiovascular health and insulin sensitivity with regular consumption. Clinically, this product is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid liquid sugar absorption and low fibre content, and is of moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to added sugar and saturated fat. It is commonly consumed as a ready-to-drink beverage in Singapore and Southeast Asia, often available in single-serve cartons of approximately 200 ml. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low category) based on analogous sweetened plant-based milk beverages, with a glycemic load of approximately 4.7 per 200 g serving.

Is coconut milk drink suitable for hypertension?

Low sugar coconut milk nutrition facts

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F01020004) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central coconut milk fatty acid profile as a secondary cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Overall confidence is high for macros and medium for fatty acid breakdown and GI.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.97 g/100 g) is estimated by applying the known coconut milk fatty acid ratio (~86% saturated) to total fat (4.62 g × 0.86 ≈ 3.97 g), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170172 (coconut milk, canned). Monounsaturated fat (0.20 g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.05 g) are similarly estimated from the same USDA reference. Trans fat is confirmed 0.0 g by product formulation for plant-based coconut milk. 2. Calcium (0.0 mg/100 g) and iron (0.0 mg/100 g) are reported as 0 in the HPB source record; these are confirmed zero values per the HPB Foodo database entry — this product is not calcium-fortified. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg as this is a plant-based product containing no animal-derived cholesterol. 3. Potassium is estimated at 90 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central coconut milk beverage reference, as HPB does not report potassium for this entry. 4. Vitamin and mineral fields (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific flavoured reduced-sugar coconut milk product are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 5. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Calcium_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — product is not fortified with calcium). Iron_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). These zero values are confirmed per the HPB Foodo database and are not data gaps.
